narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—which stands out as stronger than the typical 3.21 average among past students—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model designed to forecast first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours. The model assessed your overall academic record, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 5th (slightly below the average of 5.71), your Year 3 decile of 3rd (well below the typical 5.53), your middle family income bracket, your LSAT score of 24 (compared to the average 36.7), and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It ultimately predicted a 42.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, a risk level that triggered the rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by highlighting your college-level academic strength. However, your LSAT score exerted an equally powerful counterforce, the most significant driver toward failure in this decision because it fell considerably short of what's typical for successful bar takers. Your Year 1 decile ranking played a smaller but still notable role in favoring passage, as it showed you holding steady in the middle of your class early on. In comparison to typical students who pass, your profile aligns well on GPA and enrollment status but lags notably on LSAT and later-year performance, tipping the balance toward concern. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male student from a middle-income background—many with similar starts have succeeded—but the model prioritizes these predictors based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the 36.7 average, as it could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Reach out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback, and explore bridge programs or alternative law schools that weigh holistic factors beyond these metrics. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile and reapply stronger next cycle. Your solid GPA is already a foundation many would envy—build on it.
